hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz GLE C167 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the GLE C167 coupé SUV — from the 2.0-litre four-cylinder to the 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 monsters. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the OM656 straight-six that defines the diesel range. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes-Benz GLE C167 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the GLE C167 coupé SUV specifically — across the full 2019–2026 production run, from the entry-level GLE 350 d to the range-topping AMG GLE 63 S.

overview.intro: The Mercedes-Benz GLE C167 is the coupé SUV variant of the third-generation GLE-Class, introduced in 2019 as the successor to the C292. Built on the same V167 platform as the standard GLE SUV, the C167 brings a more dramatic roofline, sportier styling and a driving experience that prioritises on-road dynamics. Engine choice spans the OM656 2.9-litre straight-six diesel (GLE 350 d/400 d), the M256 3.0-litre inline-six petrol with EQ Boost (AMG GLE 53), the M177 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 (AMG GLE 63/63 S), plus four-cylinder options including the M264 petrol and M274 plug-in hybrid. A facelift in 2023 introduced the OM654 2.0-litre diesel (GLE 300 d), the M256.925 2.5-litre six-cylinder (GLE 450) and the OM656 370 bhp diesel (GLE 450 d).
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz V167 (C167 coupé SUV variant) • Predecessor: C292 GLE Coupé • Successor: — (current generation) • Sibling body styles in this generation: V167 SUV.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The C167 coupé SUV attracts strong specialist interest in the UK used market, with OM656 diesel and M256 petrol enquiries dominating pre-purchase inspections [PATTERN DATA].

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode applied — compressed to a single paragraph. Best & Worst Engines: The C167's engine range spans four distinct powertrain families. At the top for everyday usability sits the OM656 3.0-litre straight-six diesel — the GLE 400 d (330 bhp) offers effortless torque and proven durability in the wider Mercedes lineup, making it the most sensible choice for high-mileage buyers. The GLE 350 d (272 bhp) is a more affordable entry point with the same robust architecture. Among petrols, the M256 3.0-litre inline-six in the AMG GLE 53 (435 bhp) is a masterpiece — smooth, responsive with EQ Boost assistance, and significantly more affordable to run than its V8 stablemate. The M264 2.0-litre turbo (GLE 350) offers the lowest running costs but lacks the effortless delivery of the six-cylinder cars. The M274 plug-in hybrid (GLE 350 e) is a niche proposition — fuel economy is strong but the complexity of the hybrid system adds potential failure points and higher replacement costs. The M177 in the AMG GLE 63 and 63 S (571–612 bhp) is brutal and addictive, but demands deep pockets for fuel, tyres and specialist maintenance. For most buyers, the GLE 400 d (OM656) or AMG GLE 53 (M256) represent the sweet spot between capability, economy and running costs. Who it's for: buyers who want the sweet spot between coupé SUV presence and running-cost sanity.

rows.commonMajorFailure: AdBlue system, EGR cooler, injector wear

rows.ownershipVerdict: Premium Diesel — more power, same reliability

economicsRule.title: The C167 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,500–£7,000 OM656 recon against a £30,000–£60,000 C167 sits at just 7.5–23% of the car's value — well within Mercedes-Benz's 70% threshold. Even a £9,000–£16,000 M177 recon on a £50,000–£80,000 AMG GLE 63 sits at 11–32%. The GLE Coupé's strong retained values mean engine replacement is almost always financially viable. For OM654 four-cylinder diesels, the economics are even more favourable — a £4,000 recon on a £35,000 example is just 11%. Value the car, but know that the C167 holds its value well.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C167 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: GLE 350 d (OM656) — excellent fuel economy, proven durability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C167 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: AMG GLE 63 S (M177) — extreme performance, extreme running costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C167 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: GLE 400 d — the sweet spot between performance and running costs.

problems.title: OM656 AdBlue System & EGR Cooler Issues
problems.affectedModels: GLE 350 d, GLE 400 d, GLE 450 d (OM656.929) — 2019–2026 production.
problems.rootCause: The OM656's AdBlue system (SCR) can suffer from crystallisation in the injector and NOx sensor failures. EGR cooler leaks are also documented, particularly on higher-mileage examples. These are common modern diesel issues rather than design flaws specific to the OM656, but they can be costly to rectify if left unaddressed.
problems.recommendation: Have the AdBlue system and EGR cooler inspected at every major service — a £600–£1,000 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,000 replacement that follows a full system failure.

cta.label: Compare C167 OM656 AdBlue repair quotes →

problems.title: M256/M177 Coolant Leaks (Thermostat Housing & Turbo Lines)
problems.affectedModels: AMG GLE 53 (M256), AMG GLE 63/63 S (M177) — 2019–2026 production.
problems.rootCause: The hot-vee design of the M256 and M177 concentrates heat between the cylinder banks, accelerating gasket and seal degradation on the thermostat housing and turbocharger coolant lines. Coolant loss can lead to overheating if not addressed promptly.
problems.recommendation: Monitor coolant levels regularly on any M256 or M177-engined C167 — an £800–£1,400 thermostat housing replacement is cheap insurance against a £3,500+ engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare C167 M256/M177 coolant system repair quotes →

problems.title: M274 PHEV Battery Degradation & Hybrid System Issues
problems.rootCause: The plug-in hybrid system's battery pack can degrade over time, reducing electric range and potentially triggering warning lights. The hybrid control system can also develop faults, leading to reduced performance or limp-home mode.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a GLE 350 e, have the hybrid battery health checked by a specialist — an £800–£1,400 preventative check is dramatically cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows a complete battery failure.

cta.label: Compare C167 PHEV battery repair quotes →

problems.title: M177 Turbocharger & Injector Wear (AMG GLE 63/63 S)
problems.affectedModels: AMG GLE 63, AMG GLE 63 S (M177.980) — 2020–2026 production.
problems.rootCause: The M177 produces up to 612 bhp from 4.0 litres — an extreme specific output that places significant stress on the turbochargers, injectors and cooling system. Cars that are driven hard can experience premature turbo wear and injector failure.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying an AMG GLE 63 or 63 S, full service history is essential — a £1,200–£2,200 preventative turbo inspection is far cheaper than the £8,000+ replacement that follows a failure.

cta.label: Compare C167 M177 turbo repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: C167 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz GLE C167 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned C167 engine typically costs between £3,200 and £16,000 supply only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], depending on engine — the OM654 diesel sits at £3,200–£5,500, the OM656 at £4,000–£7,500, the M256 at £4,500–£8,500, and the M177 in the AMG GLE 63/63 S at £8,000–£16,000. Fitting adds 10–22 hours of labour at specialist rates — budget an additional £650–£1,430 for installation. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz GLE C167 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The OM656 3.0-litre straight-six diesel (GLE 350 d/400 d/450 d) is widely regarded as the most durable C167 engine — Mercedes' modern straight-six diesel architecture, proven in S-Class, GLE and G-Class applications, offering strong fuel economy and excellent longevity. Among petrols, the M256 3.0-litre inline-six in the AMG GLE 53 is well-regarded for its refinement and durability.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz GLE C167?
items.answer: AdBlue system and EGR cooler issues on the OM656 and OM654 diesels, coolant leaks from the thermostat housing and turbo lines on the M256/M177 petrols, battery degradation and hybrid system issues on the M274 PHEV (GLE 350 e), and turbocharger/injector wear on the AMG GLE 63/63 S are the most documented issues. Each requires different preventative strategies.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz GLE C167 reliable?
items.answer: Yes — the C167 represents Mercedes' modern engineering with well-proven engine architectures across the range. The OM656 diesel and M256 petrol are generally reliable with routine maintenance. The M177 in the AMG GLE 63/63 S is a high-performance engine that demands more frequent servicing and specialist attention, but it's well-proven in the wider AMG lineup. The M274 PHEV is a more complex proposition — hybrid systems can add potential failure points.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz GLE C167 engine should I avoid?
items.answer: No engine outright — all C167 engines are robust and well-engineered. However, the AMG GLE 63 S (M177) is a specialist proposition with significantly higher running costs — fuel, tyres, servicing and insurance are all in a different league. The GLE 350 e (PHEV) is a niche choice — it offers strong economy but the hybrid system adds complexity and potential battery replacement costs.

items.question: How do I know if my C167 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: AdBlue warning lights or poor running (OM656/OM654 diesel), coolant loss (M256/M177 petrols), battery degradation or hybrid warning lights (M274 PHEV), turbocharger whine or loss of power (M177), or blue smoke from the exhaust are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a C167 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: The GLE Coupé's strong retained values mean engine replacement is almost always financially viable. A £4,500–£7,000 OM656 recon on a £30,000–£60,000 example is just 7.5–23% of the car's value — well within our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. Even a £9,000–£16,000 M177 recon on a £50,000–£80,000 AMG GLE 63 is just 11–32%. Replacement is almost always justified.

items.question: How long do Mercedes-Benz GLE C167 engines last?
items.answer: The OM656 diesel can comfortably exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing. The M256 petrol is similarly durable, though coolant system maintenance is key. The M177 in the AMG GLE 63/63 S is robust for its output but will demand more frequent maintenance, particularly on cars that are driven hard. The M274 PHEV's lifespan is tied to battery health — the engine itself is proven but the hybrid system requires periodic checks.

items.question: What is the difference between the C167 and V167?
items.answer: Both are third-generation GLE-Class models sharing the V167 platform and most engines. The C167 is the coupé SUV variant (2019–2026, this page) with a more dramatic roofline and sportier styling, while the V167 is the standard SUV body style. Costs vary slightly by body style — see the V167 generation page for specifics.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz GLE C167 engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on OM656/OM654/M264/M274/M256/M177 units. Tell us your variant and approximate year and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.
